questions.question:
Consider the circuit shown below where the gates may have propagation delays. Assume that all signal transitions occur instantaneously and that wires have no delays.

Circuit: Input X feeds into both a NOT gate (inverter) and directly into an AND gate. The output of the NOT gate also feeds into the AND gate. The output of the AND gate is Y.

Which of the following statements about the circuit is/are CORRECT?

A. With no propagation delays, the output Y is always logic Zero
B. With no propagation delays, the output Y is always logic One
C. With propagation delays, the output Y can have a transient logic One after X transitions from logic Zero to logic One
D. With propagation delays, the output Y can have a transient logic Zero after X transitions from logic One to logic Zero
questions.to_find: Which statements about the AND gate circuit (Y = X AND NOT X) are correct, with and without propagation delays

understand.plain: The circuit computes Y = X AND (NOT X). Without any delays, NOT X is always the complement of X, so X AND NOT X = 0 always. However, when there is a propagation delay in the NOT gate, at the moment X changes from 0 to 1, the NOT gate output briefly stays at 1 before switching to 0. During this brief window, both inputs to the AND gate are 1, causing a glitch (momentary 1) at Y.

keywords.explain: The finite time a gate takes to produce its output after its input changes.
keywords.example: If a NOT gate has 5 ns delay, its output changes 5 ns after the input changes.

keywords.explain: A momentary unwanted output transition that occurs due to unequal propagation delays in combinational logic.
keywords.example: Y = X AND NOT X should always be 0, but a brief 1 can appear when X goes 0->1 if NOT gate is slower.

keywords.explain: When the output should remain 0 but momentarily goes to 1 due to different signal path delays.
keywords.example: Y = X AND NOT X: should be 0 always, glitches to 1 briefly when X transitions 0 to 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='180' viewBox='0 0 420 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='180' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='30' y='95' class='lbl ink'>X</text><line x1='55' y1='90' x2='100' y2='70' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='55' y1='90' x2='100' y2='120' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='100' y='108' width='60' height='24' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='125' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>NOT</text><line x1='160' y1='120' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='120' x2='220' y2='100' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='70' x2='220' y2='80' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='220' y='72' width='70' height='46' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='255' y='100' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>AND</text><line x1='290' y1='95' x2='370' y2='95' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='375' y='99' class='lbl ink'>Y</text><text x='130' y='150' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>NOT gate (delay)</text><text x='255' y='155' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Y = X AND NOT X</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Circuit diagram: input X splits into two paths — one goes directly to AND gate, other passes through NOT gate to AND gate. Output Y = X AND NOT(X).

given.aim: Determine the correct statements about the output behaviour of Y = X AND NOT(X) with and without propagation delays.

terms.meaning: The AND gate receives X on one input and the inverted X on the other input.
terms.example: If X = 1 then NOT(X) = 0, so Y = 1 AND 0 = 0. If X = 0 then NOT(X) = 1, so Y = 0 AND 1 = 0.
terms.connects: Without delays, Y is always 0 regardless of X.

terms.meaning: The NOT gate takes a small but nonzero time delta to invert its input.
terms.example: If X changes 0->1 at time t, NOT(X) stays at 1 until time t+delta.
terms.connects: During the window [t, t+delta], both X=1 and NOT(X)=1, so AND output = 1 (glitch).

terms.meaning: A hazard where the output should stay 0 but briefly goes to 1.
terms.example: Y = X AND NOT X should be 0 always, but glitches to 1 momentarily.
terms.connects: Confirms option C is correct: a transient 1 can appear when X goes 0->1.

given.plan: First check Y = X AND NOT X without delays — always 0 (option A correct, B wrong). Then consider the effect of NOT gate propagation delay when X transitions 0->1: brief window where both inputs to AND are 1, causing transient 1 output (option C correct). When X transitions 1->0, NOT gate is slow so its output stays at 0 briefly, AND output remains 0 — no glitch to 0 (option D wrong, since Y is already 0).

steps.title: Analyze without propagation delays
steps.apply: Without any delay, NOT(X) is always the exact complement of X. So Y = X AND NOT(X) = 0 for all values of X. This is a tautological contradiction — the output is always logic 0. Statement A is CORRECT, Statement B is WRONG.
steps.note: This is essentially a static function: Y = X AND NOT X = 0, equivalent to a constant-0 function.

steps.title: Analyze with propagation delay when X transitions 0 -> 1
steps.apply: Suppose X changes from 0 to 1 at time t. The direct path to the AND gate sees X = 1 immediately. However, the NOT gate takes time delta to respond, so NOT(X) stays at 1 until time t + delta. During [t, t + delta]: both AND inputs = 1, so Y = 1. After t + delta: NOT(X) goes to 0, Y returns to 0. This transient 1 glitch confirms Statement C is CORRECT.
steps.note: This is a static-0 hazard: the output should remain 0 but glitches to 1 due to unequal delays.

steps.title: Analyze with propagation delay when X transitions 1 -> 0
steps.apply: When X changes from 1 to 0 at time t: direct path to AND sees X = 0 immediately, so AND output = 0 immediately (AND with one input = 0 is always 0). The NOT gate is still computing; its output doesn't matter since AND with 0 is 0. No glitch to 0 — Y was already 0 and remains 0. Statement D is WRONG.
steps.note: When X goes 1->0, the AND gate's direct input goes to 0 first, immediately forcing Y=0. No hazard in this direction.

solution.result: Correct statements: A and C. Without propagation delays Y is always logic Zero. With propagation delays, Y can have a transient logic One after X transitions from Zero to One.

questions.formula_note: Combinational hazard analysis: Y = X AND NOT(X) = 0 always (no delay); static-0 hazard (transient 1 glitch) when X: 0->1 due to NOT gate propagation delay.

questions.question:
How many 3-to-8 line decoders with an enable input are needed to construct a 6-to-64 line decoder without using any other logic gates?

A. 7
B. 8
C. 9
D. 10
questions.to_find: Number of 3-to-8 decoders with enable needed to build a 6-to-64 decoder

understand.plain: A 6-to-64 decoder takes 6 input bits and activates exactly 1 of 64 output lines. We can build this hierarchically using 3-to-8 decoders. The upper 3 input bits select which group of 8 outputs to activate, and the lower 3 bits select which output within that group. One decoder decodes the upper 3 bits and uses its 8 outputs as enable signals for 8 more decoders that each handle the lower 3 bits.

keywords.explain: Takes 3 inputs and produces 8 outputs (only one is active), but only works when enable = 1; all outputs are 0 when enable = 0.
keywords.example: Enable=1, inputs=010 → output line 2 goes high; Enable=0 → all outputs low.

keywords.explain: Building a larger decoder by cascading smaller decoders: the first level selects a group, the second level selects within the group.
keywords.example: 6-to-64: first 3 bits pick one of 8 groups (via 1 decoder), last 3 bits pick one of 8 within the group (via 8 decoders).

x='175' y='32' width='80' height='36' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='215' y='55' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>3-to-8 #1</text><rect x='175' y='92' width='80' height='36' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='215' y='115' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>3-to-8 #k</text><rect x='175' y='152' width='80' height='36' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='215' y='175' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>3-to-8 #8</text><text x='270' y='55' class='sub ink' text-anchor='start'> → 8 outputs</text><text x='270' y='115' class='sub ink' text-anchor='start'> → 8 outputs</text><text x='270' y='175' class='sub ink' text-anchor='start'> → 8 outputs</text><text x='20' y='165' class='sub ink'>A[2:0]</text><line x1='60' y1='163' x2='175' y2='50' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3,3'/><text x='160' y='210' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Total = 1 + 8 = 9 decoders</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-level hierarchy: 1 decoder at level 1 takes upper 3 bits and produces 8 enable signals. 8 decoders at level 2 each take lower 3 bits and one enable signal, producing 8 outputs each (8x8 = 64 total).

given.aim: Count the minimum number of 3-to-8 decoders with enable needed to implement a 6-to-64 decoder.

terms.meaning: Takes 6 input address bits and activates exactly 1 of 64 output lines.
terms.example: Input 000000 activates output 0; input 111111 activates output 63.
terms.connects: 6 bits split into two groups of 3 bits each for the two-level hierarchy.

terms.meaning: One 3-to-8 decoder that decodes the upper 3 address bits (A5, A4, A3).
terms.example: Input A[5:3] = 101 activates output line 5 of level 1, which becomes the enable for decoder #5 at level 2.
terms.connects: Produces 8 enable signals, one for each level-2 decoder.

terms.meaning: Eight 3-to-8 decoders, each receiving the lower 3 bits (A2, A1, A0) and one enable signal from level 1.
terms.example: Only the decoder with its enable=1 (from level 1) is active and decodes A[2:0] to produce one of 8 outputs.

given.plan: Split the 6 input bits into two groups of 3. Use 1 decoder for the upper 3 bits — its 8 outputs serve as enable signals. Use 8 decoders for the lower 3 bits (one per enable signal). Total = 1 + 8 = 9 decoders.

steps.title: Determine the decoder hierarchy
steps.apply: A 6-to-64 decoder has 6 input bits and 64 output lines. A 3-to-8 decoder handles 3 bits. We split the 6 bits into: upper 3 bits (A5, A4, A3) and lower 3 bits (A2, A1, A0). The upper bits select which group of 8 outputs is active; the lower bits select which output within that group.

steps.title: Count decoders at each level
steps.apply: Level 1: 1 decoder takes upper 3 bits (A5, A4, A3). Its 8 output lines serve as the 8 individual enable inputs for level 2. Level 2: 8 decoders, each receiving the same lower 3 bits (A2, A1, A0), but each enabled by a different level-1 output. Only one level-2 decoder is enabled at any time (the one whose enable is high). That decoder activates one of its 8 outputs. Total outputs = 8 × 8 = 64. Total decoders = 1 + 8 = 9.
steps.note: The enable input of each level-2 decoder is critical — it ensures only one decoder is active, preventing multiple outputs from being simultaneously asserted.

solution.result: 9 decoders (1 at level 1 + 8 at level 2). Answer: C. 9

questions.formula_note: Hierarchical decoder construction: to build a (m+n)-to-2^(m+n) decoder, use 1 decoder for m bits (level 1) and 2^m decoders for n bits (level 2). Total = 1 + 2^m decoders.

questions.question: If there are m input lines and n output lines for a decoder that is used to uniquely address a byte in a 1 KB RAM, the minimum value of m + n is _______.
questions.to_find: Minimum value of m + n, where m = address inputs and n = outputs of a decoder that uniquely addresses bytes in 1 KB RAM

understand.plain: 1 KB of RAM has 1024 bytes, each of which must be uniquely addressed. A decoder takes m address bits as input and activates exactly 1 of n output lines to select a memory location. To address 1024 distinct byte locations, we need 2^m = 1024, giving m = 10. The decoder then has n = 2^m = 1024 output lines. So m + n = 10 + 1024 = 1034.

keywords.meaning: Each addressable unit in memory is 1 byte (8 bits). 1 KB = 1024 bytes = 1024 distinct addressable locations.
keywords.example: Addresses 0 to 1023 in a 1 KB RAM, each holding 1 byte.

keywords.meaning: A decoder with m input address lines and n = 2^m output lines, exactly one of which is activated for each input combination.
keywords.example: A 3-to-8 decoder: m=3, n=8, activates one of 8 output lines.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='180' viewBox='0 0 400 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='180' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><rect x='130' y='50' width='140' height='80' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='80' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Decoder</text><text x='200' y='100' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>m inputs</text><text x='200' y='118' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>n = 2^m outputs</text><line x1='50' y1='90' x2='130' y2='90' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='70' class='sub ink'>m = 10</text><text x='20' y='88' class='sub ink'>address</text><text x='20' y='104' class='sub ink'>lines</text><line x1='270' y1='90' x2='350' y2='90' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='355' y='80' class='sub ink'>n = 1024</text><text x='355' y='96' class='sub ink'>outputs</text><text x='100' y='160' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>1 KB = 1024 bytes</text><text x='280' y='160' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>m+n = 10+1024 = 1034</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Decoder block with m=10 input address lines on the left and n=1024 output lines on the right, selecting one byte location in 1 KB RAM.

given.aim: Find the minimum m + n for a decoder that uniquely addresses each byte in 1 KB RAM.

terms.connects: Requires 2^10 = 1024 unique address combinations, so the decoder needs n = 1024 output lines.

terms.meaning: The address bits fed into the decoder. Must satisfy 2^m >= number of locations.
terms.connects: m = 10 is the minimum number of address inputs needed.

terms.meaning: The number of output lines of the decoder, which equals 2^m for a fully decoded address.
terms.example: With m = 10 inputs: n = 2^10 = 1024 outputs, one per byte location.
terms.connects: n = 1024 so each output selects exactly one byte in RAM.

given.plan: Compute m from the RAM size (2^m = 1024, m = log_2(1024) = 10), then n = 2^m = 1024. Finally compute m + n = 10 + 1024 = 1034.

steps.title: Find number of input address lines m
steps.apply: 1 KB RAM = 1024 bytes = 2^10 bytes. Each byte has a unique address. To generate 1024 unique select signals, the decoder needs m input address lines where 2^m = 1024. Therefore m = log_2(1024) = 10.
steps.note: m = 10 is the minimum — using fewer bits would not generate enough unique addresses; using more is unnecessary.

steps.title: Find number of output lines n
steps.apply: A decoder with m = 10 inputs has n = 2^10 = 1024 output lines. Each output line corresponds to one unique byte address (0 to 1023). Exactly one output is asserted for each input combination.
steps.note: n must equal the number of memory locations to ensure unique addressing.

steps.title: Compute m + n
steps.note: This is the minimum because m = 10 is the minimum address bits and n = 1024 is fixed by 2^m.

questions.formula_note: For a decoder addressing 2^m locations: m = log_2(number of locations), n = 2^m output lines. For 1 KB = 2^10 bytes: m = 10, n = 1024, m + n = 1034.
